deploy.yml
=== deploy workflow triggers ===
name: Deploy

# Manual-only for now (workflow_dispatch): apply-ready scaffolding that never
# fires on its own until the Azure resources exist + secrets/vars are set. Once
# the infra is provisioned (see deploy/README.md), flip on the push trigger:
#   push:
#     branches: [main]
on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      image_tag:
        description: "Image tag to build + deploy (blank → the commit SHA, an immutable tag)"
        default: ""

concurrency:
  group: deploy-production
  cancel-in-progress: false

# OIDC federated login to Azure (no stored client secret). The federated cred +
# the listed vars/secrets must be configured first — see deploy/README.md.
# packages:write lets the GITHUB_TOKEN push the backend image to GHCR (ADR 0023).
permissions:
  id-token: write
  contents: read
  packages: write

env:
  # GHCR registry (ADR 0023) — public packages, lowercase owner. ACA pulls them
  # anonymously, so no registry credential is stored on the apps/job.
  #
  # Default the tag to the commit SHA (#382): `latest` is mutable, so a push
  # racing the deploy could make the migrate job, API, worker, and frontend each
  # resolve a *different* image; the SHA tag is immutable, so all roll the exact
  # build this run produced. A manual `image_tag` input still overrides it. On a
  # push:[main] trigger `inputs` is empty, so it falls back to the SHA too. Backend
  # + frontend share the one tag.
  IMAGE: ghcr.io/theurgicduke771/dataq-backend:${{ inputs.image_tag || github.sha }}
  IMAGE_FRONTEND: ghcr.io/theurgicduke771/dataq-frontend:${{ inputs.image_tag || github.sha }}

jobs:

content.text:
## OTel migration — done end-to-end ✅

The observability portability work (#524 + #589) is fully shipped and live-verified. This completes your directive ("start with OTel logs and SecretStore.delete (#372)" — #372 was already done, OTel now is too).

### What landed
| Piece | Outcome |
|---|---|
| **#524** opencensus → OTel logs | `AzureLogHandler` replaced with OTel `LoggerProvider` + `AzureMonitorLogExporter` behind a new shared `otel.py` seam. Redaction preserved on **both** body and exported attributes (stricter than opencensus — closed an `extra=` leak). opencensus + the whole `#405 createLock` hardening dropped; fork-safe via the SDK's `register_at_fork`. |
| **#589** generic OTLP endpoint | `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT` adds an OTLP/HTTP backend for spans **and** logs; Azure is now one impl behind the seam (ADR 0010); both can run at once. |

### Process
- **PR #628** — 2 clean commits + a review-fixup commit. Full local gate green (1409 tests, 98.4%; changed modules 96–100%).
- **Agentic review** (code-reviewer + silent-failure-hunter): no Critical/Important; folded in a real **HIGH** — isolated telemetry setup in try/except so a bad OTLP endpoint/SDK drift degrades to stdout instead of crashing startup (the #405 blast radius). Also fixed the root-logger export-failure feedback loop, added a confirmation log, and dropped dead code. Merged after 14/14 CI green.

### Parity verified (the exit gate)
- **Local OTel Collector** — received spans **and** logs over the wire; `token=<redacted>` body + `password=<redacted>` attribute, no raw secret.
- **Azure App Insights** (post-deploy) — `traces` now carry `cloud_RoleName=dataq-api`/`dataq-worker` (the OTel `service.name`); a marker probe request traced end-to-end in both `traces` (logs) and `requests` (spans). The old opencensus `celery`/`uvicorn` role rows are trailing off.

Prod deployed (run 28750735033), no config drift (prod stays App-Insights-only; OTLP endpoint unset). Tracker updated (**W2 6/14**, open issues 62) via #629; epic #597 auto-ticked.

**W2 remaining:** #532 (dry-run UC + flat-file), #584 (MCP NL spot-check), and the alerting-hardening batch (#386–#389, #416). Want me to pick up the next one?

Auth seam: DataQ-issued API keys / service tokens (REST + MCP) — provider-neutral identity
## Why

Today authentication is **exclusively Azure AD bearer tokens** (delegated/SSO) for both the REST API and `/mcp` — there is no DataQ-native credential. This is the **deepest remaining vendor lock-in** in the system and cuts against ADR [0010](../docs/adr/0010-provider-agnostic-infrastructure-seams.md) / [0013](../docs/adr/0013-marketplace-distribution-and-anti-lock-in.md): the `get_current_user` seam exists, but it has exactly *one* real implementation and the persistence layer is still Azure-shaped (`users.aad_object_id`). A BYOL customer on AWS/GCP (the ADR 0013 scenario) has no Azure

content.text:
Confirmed on both. Here's the honest status:

## You're right — both are backend-only; neither has a UI

| Feature | Backend | Frontend | Tracked? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **dbt connection** (#611) | ✅ `dbt` is a full connection type + `OrchestrationProvider` + adapter, mounted and live-verified via webhook | ❌ `CONNECTION_TYPES` in the UI stops at adf/airflow — `dbt` was never added to the source picker | ❌ no open issue |
| **DataQ PATs** (#461) | ✅ `POST/GET/DELETE /api/v1/api_keys` mounted + working (verified via REST/CLI) | ❌ `Profile.tsx` has no token section — no create/list/revoke UI at all | ❌ no open issue |

**Why this happened:** both shipped as backend/seam work and were "live-verified" through the **API and MCP directly** (curl + PAT bearer), never through the browser. So the tracker's "SHIPPED + LIVE" was true for the backend/API surface but the frontend was never built. That's a real gap in how they were closed — the UI half was silently skipped, not deferred-on-purpose.

**So: no, they are not currently being added** — nothing schedules them.

Neither is a huge task (both are additive frontend work against endpoints/types that already exist):
- **dbt UI**: add `dbt` to `CONNECTION_TYPES` + the "Orchestration" source category/blurb + a form spec for its fields (project name, artifacts store adls/s3/file, artifacts-read secret). *Note:* dbt goes under **Orchestration** (monitor/trigger), same as ADF/Airflow — not as a checkable datasource (that would violate the CLAUDE.md §4 rule).
- **PAT UI**: a "Personal Access Tokens" section on the Profile page — list (name/prefix/expiry/last-used), create (name + expiry → show-once value), revoke — plus a small `api/apiKeys.ts` client.

How do you want to proceed?

content.text:
I've completed the sweep. Here's the full picture of what the UI is missing versus what the backend supports.

## UI audit — backend capability vs. frontend

**Genuine gaps (backend shipped, no UI):**

| Gap | Detail |
|---|---|
| **dbt connection** (#611) | Not in the connection source picker at all. Should sit under **Orchestration** (like ADF/Airflow). |
| **DataQ PATs** (#461) | No token section on the Profile page. `POST/GET/DELETE /api/v1/api_keys` all exist and work. |
| **Stale "coming in Week 6" stub** | `RunNowPanel.tsx` ships a *disabled* "Teams channel (coming in Week 6)" selector with a comment saying "no notification backend exists yet." That's obsolete — the whole ResultPublisher/Teams/per-suite alerting shipped in Week 6 (#366–#378). It misleads users into thinking alerting doesn't exist. Remove or wire it. |
| **Slack + email alert channels** (#413) | The per-suite `NotificationsPanel` only exposes a **Teams** webhook. Slack + email are workspace-level env/KV config with **no UI** — arguably by-design (they're env-configured like the workspace Teams webhook), but there's no screen to even *see* which channels are active. Ambiguous; needs a decision, not necessarily a build. |

**Verified present (no gap):** monitor kinds freshness/volume (#437), orchestration pipeline-runs view + webhook-config surface, trigger bindings, check version history, dry-run preview, column-redaction policy, sharing, admin centre.

**Correctly deferred (honestly labelled, not gaps):** the check editor's `schema_drift` / `anomaly` / `comparison` cards show a `v1.x` tag + "Auto-monitor — coming soon" — those are ADR 0012 reserved kinds, scheduled for v1.1 W4–5 (#592/#593).

## Plan

Per your "file + build both" call, and folding in the two new finds:

1. **File 4 issues** — dbt-UI, PAT-UI, the stale RunNowPanel stub, and a Slack/email-UI decision issue.
2. **Build** dbt connection UI (own PR) and PAT Profile UI (own PR) — each reviewed/gated/deployed.
3. **Fix** the stale RunNowPanel stub as a small cleanup (cheap, folds into the alerting story).
4. **Slack/email**: I'll file it as a question rather than build blind — do you want per-suite Slack/email in the notifications panel, or is env-only intentional?

Let me file the issues now, then start building.

result.output:
1	"""Personal access tokens (PATs) — mint / list / revoke (ADR 0026 phase 1, #461).
2	
3	User-scoped: every route operates on the caller's own keys. The plaintext token
4	appears exactly once, in the creation response; list/read return metadata only
5	(prefix, expiry, revocation, last-used). Revocation is a soft mark
6	(`revoked_at`), keeping the row for audit.
7	"""
8	
9	import uuid
10	from datetime import datetime
11	from typing import Annotated
12	
13	from fastapi import APIRouter, Depends, status
14	from pydantic import ConfigDict, Field
15	from sqlalchemy.orm import Session
16	
17	from backend.app.api.v1._base import ApiModel
18	from backend.app.core.auth import get_current_user
19	from backend.app.db.models import User
20	from backend.app.db.session import get_db
21	from backend.app.services import api_key_service as svc
22	
23	router = APIRouter(tags=["auth"])
24	
25	
26	class ApiKeyCreate(ApiModel):
27	    name: str = Field(min_length=1, max_length=128, description="Label, e.g. 'ci-smoke'")
28	    expires_in_days: int = Field(
29	        default=svc.DEFAULT_EXPIRY_DAYS,
30	        ge=1,
31	        le=svc.MAX_EXPIRY_DAYS,
32	        description="Days until the key expires (no non-expiring keys)",
33	    )
34	
35	
36	class ApiKeyRead(ApiModel):
37	    model_config = ConfigDict(from_attributes=True)
38	
39	    id: uuid.UUID
40	    name: str
41	    key_prefix: str
42	    created_at: datetime
43	    expires_at: datetime
44	    revoked_at: datetime | None
45	    last_used_at: datetime | None
46	
47	
48	class ApiKeyCreated(ApiKeyRead):
49	    """Creation response — the ONLY place the plaintext token ever appears."""
50	
51	    token: str = Field(description="The API key. Shown once; store it now.")
52	
53	
54	@router.post(
55	    "/me/api-keys",
56	    response_model=ApiKeyCreated,
57	    status_code=status.HTTP_201_CREATED,
58	    summary="Mint an API key (personal access token) — plaintext shown once",
59	)
60	def create_api_key(
61	    payload: ApiKeyCreate,
62	    current_user: Annotated[User, Depends(get_current_user)],
63	    db: Annotated[Session, Depends(get_db)],
64	) -> ApiKeyCreated:
65	    """Mint a PAT that authenticates as you (`Authorization: Bearer dq_live_…`)
66	    on the REST API and `/mcp` alike, inheriting your per-suite access."""
67	    key, token = svc.create_key(
68	        db, current_user, name=payload.name, expires_in_days=payload.expires_in_days
69	    )
70	    return ApiKeyCreated(**ApiKeyRead.model_validate(key).model_dump(), token=token)
71	
72	
73	@router.get(
74	    "/me/api-keys",
75	    response_model=list[ApiKeyRead],
76	    summary="List your API keys (metadata only — never the token)",
77	)
78	def list_api_keys(
79	    current_user: Annotated[User, Depends(get_current_user)],
80	    db: Annotated[Session, Depends(get_db)],
81	) -> list[ApiKeyRead]:
82	    return [ApiKeyRead.model_validate(k) for k in svc.list_keys(db, current_user)]
83	
84	
85	@router.delete(
86	    "/me/api-keys/{key_id}",
87	    status_code=status.HTTP_204_NO_CONTENT,
88	    summary="Revoke one of your API keys",
89	)
90	def revoke_api_key(
91	    key_id: uuid.UUID,
92	    current_user: Annotated[User, Depends(get_current_user)],
93	    db: Annotated[Session, Depends(get_db)],
94	) -> None:
95	    """Revocation is immediate (the key stops authenticating) and idempotent.
96	    Another user's key 404s — indistinguishable from a nonexistent one."""
97	    svc.revoke_key(db, current_user, key_id)

content.text: There's real auto-classification machinery (`column_classification.py`, `derive_column_policy`) plus a user-settable `set_column_policy`. The key is which one actually drives sample-row redaction. Let me read the redaction path:

result.output:
484	
485	
486	def _policy_pii(column: str, policy: Mapping[str, Any] | None) -> bool:
487	    """Level 3 — the suite override explicitly lists the column as PII."""
488	    if not policy:
489	        return False
490	    listed = {str(c).strip().lower() for c in (policy.get("pii_columns") or [])}
491	    return column.strip().lower() in listed
492	
493	
494	def _policy_identifier(column: str, policy: Mapping[str, Any] | None) -> bool:
495	    """Level 3 — the suite override names the column as the shown identifier."""
496	    if not policy:
497	        return False
498	    ident = policy.get("identifier_column")
499	    return bool(ident) and str(ident).strip().lower() == column.strip().lower()
500	
501	
502	def _known_sensitive(
503	    column: str,
504	    values: Sequence[Any],
505	    policy: Mapping[str, Any] | None,
506	    tags: Mapping[str, str] | None,
507	) -> bool:
508	    """Whether a column is **known** sensitive — a governance tag (floor), an explicit
509	    override, or an *affirmative* name/value PII signal (not the conservative default).
510	    Gates the **tested** and **identifier** columns: those are shown *unless* known
511	    sensitive (seeing the failing value / locating the row is the point)."""
512	    return (
513	        _tag_sensitive(column, tags) or _policy_pii(column, policy) or is_sensitive(column, values)
514	    )
515	
516	
517	def _may_show_incidental(
518	    column: str,
519	    values: Sequence[Any],
520	    policy: Mapping[str, Any] | None,
521	    tags: Mapping[str, str] | None,
522	) -> bool:
523	    """Whether an *incidental* column (not the tested / identifier one) may be shown:
524	    only when it's affirmatively an IDENTIFIER or SAFE value — everything else
525	    default-masks (#415), so security can't regress. A governance tag / override-PII
526	    always masks; an override-named identifier shows **unless it is affirmatively PII**
527	    (a designated locator can't un-mask a column whose name/values are direct PII —
528	    e.g. an ``EMAIL`` set as identifier, or a natural key holding emails)."""
529	    if _tag_sensitive(column, tags) or _policy_pii(column, policy):
530	        return False
531	    if _policy_identifier(column, policy):
532	        return not is_sensitive(column, values)
533	    return classify_column(column, list(values)) is not ColumnClass.PII
534	
535	
536	def _values_by_column(rows: Sequence[Any]) -> dict[str, list[Any]]:
537	    """Gather each column's values across the sampled failing rows, so the classifier's
538	    value signal (emails, id-shape) sees the whole column, not one cell."""
539	    out: dict[str, list[Any]] = defaultdict(list)
540	    for row in rows:
541	        if isinstance(row, dict):
542	            for col, val in row.items():
543	                out[str(col)].append(val)
544	    return dict(out)
545	
546	
547	def _redact_row(
548	    row: Any,
549	    *,
550	    tested_column: str | None,
551	    policy: Mapping[str, Any] | None,
552	    tags: Mapping[str, str] | None,
553	    values_by_column: Mapping[str, list[Any]],
554	) -> Any:
555	    """Mask a failing-row dict per column: the tested column shows unless *known*
556	    sensitive; every other column shows only if affirmatively identifier/safe
557	    (default-mask). Non-dict rows fall back to full masking."""
558	    if not isinstance(row, dict):
559	        return _redact_sample_value(row)
560	    # Case-insensitive tested-column match: GX returns the warehouse's column casing
561	    # (Snowflake upper-cases), which need not match the check config's `column`.
562	    tested = (tested_column or "").strip().lower()
563	    out: dict[Any, Any] = {}
564	    for col, val in row.items():
565	        name = str(col)
566	        vals = values_by_column.get(name, [val])
567	        if tested and name.strip().lower() == tested:
568	            show = not _known_sensitive(name, vals, policy, tags)
569	        else:
570	            show = _may_show_incidental(name, vals, policy, tags)
571	        out[col] = val if show else _redact_sample_value(val)
572	    return out
573	
574	
575	def redact_sample_failures(
576	    sample: dict[str, Any] | None,
577	    *,
578	    tested_column: str | None = None,
579	    policy: dict[str, Any] | None = None,
580	    tags: Mapping[str, str] | None = None,
581	) -> dict[str, Any] | None:
582	    """Redact a result's `sample_failures` for safe surfacing on the read API.
583	
584	    `sample_failures` carries aggregate counts plus `partial_unexpected_list` — the
585	    failing values of the **tested column** — and (when the runner records it) an
586	    `unexpected_index_list` of failing rows. Suite-level ``view`` authz lets
587	    share-recipients read a suite's results, so PII must not cross that boundary
588	    unredacted (CLAUDE.md PII rule; purged on the retention sweep below).
589	
590	    Column-aware policy (#415) — surgical, not blanket, over three authority layers:
591	    datasource **tags** (``tags``, a governance floor — later increment), the suite
592	    **override** (``policy``), and the name+value **classifier**. Rules:
593	
594	    * numeric summary keys (`unexpected_count` / `unexpected_percent`) always pass;
595	    * `partial_unexpected_list` (the tested column's scalar failing values) passes when
596	      the tested column is **not known sensitive** — so a non-PII breach (a bad
597	      ``LINE_TOTAL``) is *visible* while a PII tested column (``email``) stays masked;
598	      with **no** ``tested_column`` the list is masked (no column context → safe default);
599	    * row-dicts (`unexpected_index_list`, or a dict-shaped `partial_unexpected_list`)
600	      are redacted **per column** — the tested column + identifiers + safe values shown,
601	      PII + unclassified masked;
602	    * everything else default-masks. ``None`` sample passes through unchanged.
603	    """
604	    if not sample:
605	        return None
606	    index_rows = sample.get("unexpected_index_list")
607	    index_vbc = _values_by_column(index_rows) if isinstance(index_rows, list) else {}
608	    out: dict[str, Any] = {}
609	    for key, value in sample.items():
610	        if _is_safe_summary(key, value):
611	            out[key] = value
612	        elif key == "unexpected_index_list" and isinstance(value, list):
613	            out[key] = [
614	                _redact_row(
615	                    row,
616	                    tested_column=tested_column,
617	                    policy=policy,
618	                    tags=tags,
619	                    values_by_column=index_vbc,
620	                )
621	                for row in value
622	            ]
623	        elif key == "partial_unexpected_list" and isinstance(value, list):
624	            if value and all(isinstance(v, dict) for v in value):
625	                vbc = _values_by_column(value)
626	                out[key] = [
627	                    _redact_row(
628	                        row,
629	                        tested_column=tested_column,
630	                        policy=policy,
631	                        tags=tags,
632	                        values_by_column=vbc,
633	                    )
634	                    for row in value
635	                ]
636	            elif tested_column is not None and not _known_sensitive(
637	                tested_column, value, policy, tags
638	            ):
639	                out[key] = value  # the tested column's failing values — surfaced
640	            else:
641	                out[key] = _redact_sample_value(value)
642	        else:
643	            out[key] = _redact_sample_value(value)

===== column_classification.py header (detection design) =====
"""Column classification for failing-sample redaction (#415).

Classifies a column as **IDENTIFIER**, **PII**, or **SAFE** from its *name* plus a
handful of *sampled values*, so the sample redactor can surface a locating
identifier and the safe tested value while masking PII — instead of blanket-masking
every value (which makes a failing-row sample unactionable: you can't see *what* was
wrong or *which* row).

This is the **name-heuristic + value-signal** layer (#415 detection precedence step
2). Datasource-native classification (Snowflake ``privacy_category`` / UC column
tags) and an explicit suite override sit *above* it; both can overrule a guess here.

Design (adapted from a name-pattern + entropy/hash value heuristic — not lifted):

* **Name tokens give the primary signal.** A column whose name carries a *person*
  token (``email``/``phone``/``first_name``…) is PII; a *non-person* id token
  (``order_number``/``sku``/``tracking_number``…) is an IDENTIFIER; a metric / time /
  status token (``load_ts``/``amount``/``status``…) is SAFE.
* **Person-linking ids are identifiers.** ``customer_id`` / ``user_id`` are
  surrogate/pseudonymous keys — the ideal row locator, and they don't leak a direct
  identifier — so they are shown. The value signal still catches a *natural* key that
  holds PII (a ``user_id`` column of emails → PII), and an explicit override / tag can
  overrule for a stricter posture.
* **Value shape refines an otherwise-unknown column.** UUID/hash-shaped,
  high-cardinality values look like identifiers; high-entropy encrypted/hashed blobs
  are treated as sensitive.
* **Conservative default.** Anything not confidently IDENTIFIER or SAFE is PII, so the
  redactor's default-mask posture (security can't regress, #415) is preserved.

Pure, dependency-light, DB-free — unit-testable in isolation and reused by the
policy-derivation path (a later step wires it to auto-fill
``Suite.column_policy``).
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import math
import re
from collections.abc import Iterable, Sequence
from enum import StrEnum
===== is_sensitive / classify_column defaults (conservative?) =====
def classify_column(name: str, sampled_values: Sequence[object] | None = None) -> ColumnClass:
    """Classify a column as IDENTIFIER / PII / SAFE for sample redaction (#415).

    Precedence:
    1. A **PII name** → PII (always mask).
    2. **Directly-sensitive values** (emails, encoded blobs) → PII — this *overrides* a
       name that looks like an identifier, so a natural key holding PII (a ``user_id``
       column of emails) is masked, not shown.
    3. The **name** signal (IDENTIFIER / SAFE) when it carried a known token.
    4. The **value** signal (IDENTIFIER) for a name-inconclusive column.
    5. Otherwise **PII** — conservative default-mask, so security never regresses.

    ``sampled_values`` are a small profile sample (a few rows); ``None``/empty falls
    back to the name signal only.
    """
    by_name = _name_signal(name)
    if by_name is ColumnClass.PII:
        return ColumnClass.PII
    by_value = _value_signal(sampled_values or [])
    if by_value is ColumnClass.PII:  # sensitive values override a name-based identifier
        return ColumnClass.PII
    if by_name is not None:  # IDENTIFIER or SAFE
        return by_name
    if by_value is not None:  # IDENTIFIER
        return by_value
    return ColumnClass.PII


def is_sensitive(name: str, sampled_values: Sequence[object] | None = None) -> bool:
    """Whether a column is **affirmatively** PII — a person/sensitive name token or a
    directly-sensitive value signal (emails, encoded blobs) — as opposed to the
    conservative *default* mask.

    This is the gate for a column that is otherwise shown (the **tested** column whose
    failing values are the point of the sample, or a designated **identifier**): show
    it *unless it is affirmatively sensitive*. Distinct from :func:`classify_column`,
    which default-masks an unrecognised column — appropriate for *incidental* columns,
    not for one the user deliberately checked or named.
    """
    return (
        _name_signal(name) is ColumnClass.PII
        or _value_signal(list(sampled_values or [])) is ColumnClass.PII
    )

content.text:
Verified end-to-end. Here's the definitive answer.

## Sample failure values — redaction is **auto-applied**, not user-gated ✅

This is the reassuring answer: **identifier/PII protection is automatic and secure-by-default — the user does *not* have to apply anything first.**

The classifier (`column_classification.py`, #415) runs on every result's failing sample **at read time**, whether or not a policy is set (`runs.py:190` → `redact_sample_failures(..., policy=suite.column_policy)`, and `policy` may be `None`). Its posture is **conservative default-mask**:

- **Tested column** → its failing values are shown (so the sample is actionable — you can see *what* was wrong) **unless** that column is affirmatively PII (e.g. an `email` check → masked).
- **Every other column** → shown only if it's affirmatively an **identifier** (e.g. `order_number`, `customer_id` — a row locator that isn't a direct identifier) or a **safe** value. **Anything else — including unclassified columns — is masked automatically.** Security can't regress by omission.
- Name **and** value signals: a `user_id` column that actually holds emails is caught by the value signal and masked, even though the name looks like an identifier.

The **suite column policy** (the `SamplePolicyPanel` you saw with "e.g. email, phone") is a purely **optional override on top**:
- `identifier_column` — pin which column shows as the row locator.
- `pii_columns` — force-mask specific columns the heuristic missed.
- An **"Auto-detect" button** that profiles the target and *suggests* a policy (via `derive_column_policy`) — but you never have to; the baseline is automatic.

There's also a **reserved third layer** — datasource governance tags (Snowflake `privacy_category` / UC column tags) as a floor above both — designed but **not yet wired** ("later increment"). That's the one latent piece here, if you want it tracked.

## Column profiler — works ✅

Functional across all 4 datasources (`ColumnProfilePanel` + `profile_service`), and it's the engine behind that "Auto-detect" policy suggestion (`derive_column_policy` is wired: `GET` endpoint at `suites.py:551` → the panel's suggest button). Only open follow-up is **#327** (N+1 query batching — performance, not correctness).

---

## Where I am

I paused the PAT-UI build after filing the four gap issues (**#630** dbt-UI, **#631** PAT-UI, **#632** stale stub, **#633** Slack/email decision) — I have the backend contract for the PAT UI ready to go.

Want me to **resume building** (PAT UI first, then dbt), or do you have more of the product you'd like me to audit first? I'm happy to keep verifying — you're finding real gaps.

For the sample records, I'm not seeing the identifier columns. It's just the column that in the gx check. And for the column profiler, I'm not seeing the drop down. It's still the text box only. Can you cross check both?

These are concrete runtime observations that may point to a real bug in what the runner captures. Let me cross-check both against the actual code — starting with what the GX runner puts into the sample (that determines whether identifier columns can appear at all):

) -> list[CheckOutcome]:
    """Run a suite's checks, dispatching by `check.kind` (ADR 0012), and return one
    outcome per check in the **same order** (so they zip 1:1 onto result rows).

    * ``expectation`` kind → the GX `CheckRunner.run_checks`.
    * ``freshness``/``volume`` (monitor kinds) → the `MonitorRunner.run_monitors`
      SQL path — only when the runner is a SQL datasource (Snowflake/UC). A monitor
      check on a flat-file runner raises (gated here, not silently mis-run).
    * any other reserved kind (`schema_drift`/`anomaly`/`comparison`) has no runner
      yet → `NotImplementedError`.

    This composes with the connection-type runner selection (ADR 0011): `kind`
    chooses the *monitor*, `connection.type` chose the *adapter* (the runner)."""
    expectation_idx = [i for i, c in enumerate(checks) if c.kind == _EXPECTATION_KIND]
    monitor_idx = [i for i, c in enumerate(checks) if c.kind in MONITOR_KINDS]
    unsupported = sorted(
        {c.kind for c in checks if c.kind != _EXPECTATION_KIND and c.kind not in MONITOR_KINDS}
    )
    if unsupported:
        raise NotImplementedError(f"no run path for check kind(s) {', '.join(unsupported)}")

    outcomes: list[CheckOutcome | None] = [None] * len(checks)
    if expectation_idx:
        specs = [
            CheckSpec(expectation_type=checks[i].expectation_type, kwargs=dict(checks[i].config))
            for i in expectation_idx
        ]
        suite_outcome = runner.run_checks(
            table=table, schema=schema, checks=specs, index_columns=index_columns
        )
        for i, oc in zip(expectation_idx, suite_outcome.checks, strict=True):
            outcomes[i] = oc

content.text:
Cross-checked both against the full code path. You're right on both counts, and I need to **correct part of my earlier answer** — I conflated two different things.

## #1 — Identifier columns in samples: **NOT automatic** (I was wrong on this)

Earlier I said "identifier/PII is auto-applied." That's true for **masking** (the classifier auto-*masks* PII with zero config). But **showing an identifier column is a separate step that the runner gates on explicit config**:

`worker/tasks.py:132-134`:
```python
policy = suite.column_policy or {}
identifier = policy.get("identifier_column")
index_columns = [str(identifier)] if identifier else None   # ← None unless you set it
```

- With **no** `identifier_column` set → `index_columns=None` → GX runs plain `COMPLETE` → returns **bare positional indices** → the runner's `_is_identifier_index_list` rejects them → the whole row list is dropped → the sample carries **only the tested column's failing values**. That's exactly what you see.
- The auto-classifier (`derive_column_policy` / "Auto-detect") only produces a *suggestion*; it is **never auto-persisted**. Nothing sets `identifier_column` for you.

**So to get identifier columns today:** open the suite's "Failing-sample columns" panel → Auto-detect (or type one) → **Save** → **re-run** the suite. Only then does GX capture that column into the rows. That's a real UX gap — the row locator is invisible out of the box, and the fix (set + save + re-run) isn't discoverable.

## #2 — "Profiler is a text box, no dropdown": two different inputs, both explain it

There are **two** column inputs, and neither gives you an obvious dropdown:

**a) The "Failing-sample columns" panel** (`SamplePolicyPanel` — identifier/PII selectors): these are `mode="tags"` Selects whose options are seeded *only from values you've already entered* — it explicitly **does not fetch the column list** (code comment: *"we don't fetch the full column list here (free-tag entry)"*). So it's genuinely free-text with no catalog dropdown. **A real gap.**

**b) The actual "Column profiler" panel** (`ColumnProfilePanel`): this one *is* meant to be a dropdown — an `AutoComplete` backed by live introspection (`GET /suites/{id}/columns`). But three things make it look like a plain text box:
- It only fetches columns **when you first expand the "Column profiler" collapse** (lazy, one live warehouse round-trip).
- If introspection returns nothing or errors (no credential / unreachable warehouse — **very relevant during the Azure wind-down**), it *silently degrades to a plain text box with no list* — by design, so profiling is never blocked.
- Even when populated, an antd `AutoComplete` renders as an input (no dropdown arrow); suggestions only appear on focus/typing — so it doesn't *look* like a Select.

So (b) being a bare text box most likely means **introspection returned nothing for that connection** (or you're on a batch/pattern target). That's testable.

## What I'd do

These are two genuine findings on top of the four I already filed. Concretely:
- **#1** → an issue: auto-persist a derived `identifier_column` on first profile/run (or at least surface a clear "set an identifier to locate rows" prompt) so samples have a locator by default.
- **#2a** → an issue: back the `SamplePolicyPanel` selectors with the same `listColumns` catalog the profiler uses (real dropdown instead of free-tags).
- **#2b** → likely not a code bug but empty introspection — I can **verify it live** by calling `GET /suites/{id}/columns` against a prod suite (I can use a DataQ PAT for this) to see whether it returns columns or errors.

Want me to (1) live-test the profiler introspection against a prod suite to confirm whether #2b is "empty introspection" vs. a bug, and (2) file the two new issues — before I get back to building the dbt/PAT UIs?

for the first issue. I was thinking the auto classifier will select the identifier and PII column list first. And if it is incorrect, user will override them in the UI. So upon creating a suite, the auto classifier will detect the identifier and PII columns. And for every check that's added in the suite, those columns will automatically added as identifier and PII. 
For the second issue both a and b, go with your suggested approach. 
Also continue with the earlier PAT, dbt, stale RunNowPanel cleanup and  Slack/email [should be same as current teams channel] issues.

Got it — clear direction on all fronts. Let me refine #1 into a concrete design, confirm #2, and lay out the full program.

**On #1 (your model — auto-classify at suite creation, override in UI):** That's the right UX. Key refinements from the code reality:
- The policy is already **suite-level**, so once derived it *automatically* applies to every check in the suite — your "for every check" requirement is satisfied for free; nothing per-check needed.
- It must be **fail-soft**: a suite with no target yet, a batch/pattern target, or an unreachable warehouse (no credential) can't be classified — those must leave the policy empty and never block suite creation.
- The **identifier column is the important auto-pick** (it's what makes GX capture a row locator). PII masking already happens automatically at redaction time regardless, so the derived `pii_columns` is a bonus, not load-bearing.
- I'll do it **async** (a background task on suite-create) so creation never blocks on a warehouse round-trip.

Let me file the two new issues, verify #2b live via App Insights (no bug vs. empty-introspection), and set up the program.

input.command:
cd /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ
MS="v1.1 Week 2 — Portability: OTel logs, secrets lifecycle, dry-run depth"

I5=$(gh issue create --label enhancement --milestone "$MS" \
  --title "Auto-derive the failing-sample column policy on suite creation (identifier + PII)" \
  --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
Today a suite's `column_policy` starts empty, and `worker/tasks.py` only asks GX to capture identifier columns when `column_policy.identifier_column` is set. So **out of the box, failing-sample rows show only the tested (GX-check) column — no row locator** — until the user manually opens the panel, runs Auto-detect, saves, and re-runs. Reported by a user.

## Desired behaviour
On **suite creation** (once it has a concrete target), auto-classify the target's columns and **persist** `column_policy` (`identifier_column` + `pii_columns`) so samples have a locator + PII masking by default. The user can then override in the existing `SamplePolicyPanel`. Because the policy is **suite-level**, it applies to every check in the suite automatically.

## Design
- Async background task `auto_classify_columns(suite_id)` dispatched post-create (never block create on a warehouse round-trip).
- Introspect column names (`listColumns` path) + classify via `column_classification.classify_column` (name signal); pick the best IDENTIFIER as `identifier_column`, the PII names as `pii_columns`; persist to `Suite.column_policy` **only if currently empty** (don't clobber a user override).
- **Fail-soft**: no target / batch-pattern target / introspection failure (no credential, unreachable) → leave policy null (today's behaviour). Never raise into suite create.
- Re-derive on target change (same task); manual Auto-detect stays.
- Note: value-signal PII (e.g. a `user_id` column holding emails) is still caught at **redaction time** regardless, so a name-only derive is a safe default.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Creating a suite with a reachable table/UC target auto-populates `identifier_column` + `pii_columns`, visible in the panel
- [ ] Failing-sample rows then include the identifier column with **no** manual setup (after a run)
- [ ] Unreachable/credential-less/batch-pattern targets leave the policy empty and never fail suite creation
- [ ] A user-set policy is never overwritten by the auto-derive
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -1)
echo "auto-classify issue: $I5"

I6=$(gh issue create --label enhancement --milestone "$MS" \
  --title "SamplePolicyPanel: back identifier/PII selectors with the column catalog (real dropdown, not free-tags)" \
  --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
`SamplePolicyPanel` renders the identifier + PII selectors as `Select mode="tags"` whose options are seeded **only from already-entered values** (code comment: "we don't fetch the full column list here (free-tag entry)"). So there's no real dropdown of the target's columns — you must free-type names or use Auto-detect. Reported by a user ("it's still the text box only").

## Fix
Back both selectors with the same `listColumns` (`GET /suites/{id}/columns`) catalog the `ColumnProfilePanel` already uses, so the user picks from the target's actual columns. Keep free-typing as a fallback (a column introspection didn't return). Degrade gracefully to free-tags when introspection is empty/unavailable (same posture as the profiler).

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Identifier + PII selectors offer the target's real columns as a dropdown
- [ ] Free-typing still works (introspection-missed columns)
- [ ] Empty/failed introspection degrades to today's free-tag behaviour, never blocks the panel
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -1)
echo "policy-panel dropdown issue: $I6"

<result>I have everything I need. I verified the diff against the actual backend and supporting frontend code.

## Review: PR #636 — PAT management UI (`feature/pat-management-ui`)

Reviewed the full `git diff main...HEAD`: `frontend/src/api/apiKeys.ts`, `frontend/src/components/profile/ApiKeysPanel.tsx`, `frontend/src/pages/Profile.tsx`, and the three test files — cross-checked against the backend (`backend/app/api/v1/api_keys.py`, `api_key_service.py`), the reference panel (`SchedulesPanel.tsx`), `useAsyncData`, the axios client, and `formatTimestamp`.

### Security verdict: token handling is correct

The credential-handling core — the thing this review most needed to confirm — is sound:

- **`token` never enters list data.** `ApiKey` (and backend `ApiKeyRead`) has no `token` field; only `createApiKey`'s `ApiKeyCreated` carries it. It's held solely in `CreateTokenModal`'s local `created` state and rendered only in the reveal view. `onCreated()` triggers a metadata-only refetch — the plaintext is never merged into the table's dataset.
- **All modal close paths clear the plaintext.** `close()` calls `reset()` (sets `created = null`) before `onClose()` on every exit — Done button, the X/`onCancel`, and Escape. `maskClosable={!created}` blocks the one path (mask click) that would bypass `onCancel`. So the token is reliably dropped from state and DOM on dismissal; it is never logged or passed to `message`/callbacks.
- **Contract is exact.** Path `/api/v1/me/api-keys` (baseURL `/api/v1` + `/me/api-keys`) matches the router. Fields `id/name/key_prefix/created_at/expires_at/revoked_at/last_used_at` (+`token` on create) match `ApiKeyRead`/`ApiKeyCreated`. Payload `{name, expires_in_days}` and the 1–365 bounds match `ApiKeyCreate` (`ge=1, le=MAX`). Frontend `PAT_DEFAULT_EXPIRY_DAYS=90`/`PAT_MAX_EXPIRY_DAYS=365` match `svc.DEFAULT_EXPIRY_DAYS`/`MAX_EXPIRY_DAYS`. `formatTimestamp(null)` renders `—`, so nullable `last_used_at`/`revoked_at` are safe. Revoke action is correctly hidden for already-revoked keys; status derivation (revoked > expired > active) is correct.

No Critical (90-100) findings. The findings below are test-quality gaps at the Important tier.

### Important (80-89)

**1. The "shown exactly once" invariant is asserted only half-way — confidence 82**
`frontend/tests/components/ApiKeysPanel.test.tsx`, test `creates a token and reveals it exactly once`.
The test asserts the token *appears* in the reveal, but never asserts it is *gone afterward*. There is no assertion that after clicking **Done** the plaintext disappears from the DOM, and none that it never appears in the token table. For a feature whose entire security premise is show-once (and in a project that "takes credential handling seriously"), the actual once-only property — `reset()` clearing `created`, and the list never carrying `token` — is the invariant most worth pinning, and it's untested. The test name overstates what it covers.
Fix: after clicking Done, assert `screen.queryByText('dq_live_ab12THE_FULL_SECRET')` is `null`; and in the list test assert the full secret never renders (only the `dq_live_ab12…` prefix).

**2. No failure-mode tests for create or revoke — confidence 80**
`frontend/tests/components/ApiKeysPanel.test.tsx`.
Only happy paths are covered. Two error branches with real logic are untested:
- `CreateTokenModal.onSubmit` catch → `message.error('Create failed: …')`, and critically that on failure `created` stays null so **no reveal is shown** (a rejected create must not flash a token view).
- `ApiKeyTable.onRevoke` catch → `message.error` **plus `throw err`** to keep the confirm modal open on failure — the deliberate keep-open behavior is asserted nowhere.
This runs against the project's own guidance (memory: "Test failure modes, not happy paths"; the reference `SchedulesPanel` has the same error branches). Add a `mockCreate.mockRejectedValue(...)` case asserting no reveal, and a `mockRevoke.mockRejectedValue(...)` case asserting the confirm stays open.

### Minor notes (below the 80 report threshold, listed for completeness)

- **`InputNumber` expiry accepts non-integer / transiently out-of-range input** (`ApiKeysPanel.tsx`, expiry field). `min={1} max={365}` clamps on blur, and the backend 422s anything invalid (surfaced via `message.error`), so it's guarded end-to-end — but adding `precision={0}` would stop a `90.5` from ever being submittable. Low impact.
- **`Spin description=` / `Alert title=` props** are non-standard antd prop names (antd uses `tip` / `message`), but this PR copies them verbatim from the already-merged `SchedulesPanel`, so it's a pre-existing codebase convention, not introduced here. Out of scope for this PR.

### Consistency

Matches `SchedulesPanel` conventions well: `App.useApp()` for `message`/`modal`, `useAsyncData` + `reload`, the loading/error/empty triad, `busyId` gating, `modal.confirm` with `throw err` keep-open, `aria-label`ed icon buttons, and `formatTimestamp` for all timestamps. The two-phase (form → reveal) single-modal design is a clean, appropriate extension of the pattern.</result>

content.text: The token `<code>` stays mounted after close — antd keeps modal content in the DOM when hidden. The correct fix is `destroyOnHidden` (also better security: the plaintext DOM node is destroyed, not just hidden). content.text: antd 6 — the codebase uses `destroyOnHidden` (unmounts content on close: token DOM node destroyed, cleaner + more secure), and `maskClosable` → `mask={{ closable }}`. Let me apply both:

input.old_string:
<Modal
      open={open}
      title={created ? 'Copy your token now' : 'New personal access token'}
      onCancel={close}
      maskClosable={!created} // once created, force an explicit acknowledge
input.new_string:
<Modal
      open={open}
      title={created ? 'Copy your token now' : 'New personal access token'}
      onCancel={close}
      // Unmount body on close so the revealed plaintext token leaves the DOM
      // entirely (not just hidden) — reinforces show-once.
      destroyOnHidden
      mask={{ closable: !created }} // once created, force an explicit acknowledge

content.text: The unhandled rejection comes from `throw err` in the revoke `modal.confirm` onOk — antd 6 doesn't swallow the onOk rejection, so it leaks (a latent issue in the copied SchedulesPanel pattern). Cleaner: surface the error toast and let the confirm close; the key stays listed for a retry. Let me fix the component and the test:

117	/** Initial `config` for a freshly-selected type (seeds the default auth_type). */
118	export function initialConfigForType(type: ConnectionType): Record<string, unknown> {
119	  const auth = CONNECTION_FORM_SPECS[type].auth;
120	  return auth ? { auth_type: auth[0].value } : {};
121	}

123	/** The auth mode a connection's config selects (undefined for single-secret types). */
124	export function activeAuthOption(
125	  type: ConnectionType,
126	  config: Record<string, unknown> | undefined,
127	): AuthOption | undefined {
128	  const auth = CONNECTION_FORM_SPECS[type].auth;
129	  if (!auth) return undefined;
130	  return auth.find((a) => a.value === config?.auth_type) ?? auth[0];
131	}

133	/**
134	 * Compose the write-only secret payload. A passphrase rides a combined JSON
135	 * payload — one SecretStore entry per connection, so rotation stays atomic
136	 * (the backend Snowflake adapter parses it; #194). Without a passphrase —
137	 * including a whitespace-only one, which is a stray keystroke, not a real
138	 * passphrase — the secret is sent as-is (bare PEM = unencrypted key, unchanged).
139	 */
140	export function composeSecret(secret: string, passphrase?: string): string {
141	  return passphrase?.trim() ? JSON.stringify({ private_key: secret, passphrase }) : secret;
142	}

content.text: The API secret is **optional** (`secret: str | None = None`) — so a `file://` dbt connection needs no credential. My plan: extend `TextField` with `type: 'tags'` (for `jobs`) and `TypeSpec` with `optionalSecret`. Let me read the remaining files to wire dbt in:

<result>I've completed the review. All 7 tests pass, typecheck-relevant exhaustiveness holds, and I verified the dbt form against the backend `DbtConfig`. This is a clean, well-scoped change.

## Review summary — PR #637 (dbt connection UI)

Reviewed the full `git diff main...HEAD` across the 6 files, cross-checked against `backend/app/orchestration/dbt.py` (`DbtConfig`), the shared form framework, and the datasource-filtering consumers. Ran the test suite (7/7 pass) and confirmed `BuildOutlined` is a real antd export.

**No findings at or above the reporting threshold (confidence ≥80). The change is safe to merge.** Details on each focus area below, plus two low-confidence notes for the author's awareness.

### 1. Regression risk to the existing six types — none (verified)
- The `type='tags'` branch in `ConfigTextField` is gated on `field.type === 'tags'` (ConnectionTypeFields.tsx:29). Only dbt's `jobs` field sets `type: 'tags'`; every other field has `type` undefined and falls through to `<Input />`. Snowflake/ADLS/S3/UC/ADF/Airflow rendering is byte-for-byte unchanged.
- The `optional` branch in `SecretField` defaults to `false` (ConnectionTypeFields.tsx:43) and is only passed `spec.optionalSecret` (line 124), which is undefined for all six existing types → `required` rule still applies. Auth-mode secrets (line 119) never pass `optional`, so key-pair/basic/token paths keep their required secret. No behavioural drift.

### 2. Correctness vs backend `DbtConfig` — matches
- `project_name`, `artifacts_uri`, `jobs`, `region`, `access_key_id` map 1:1 to the Pydantic model fields; `model_config = extra="forbid"` is satisfied (no stray keys).
- **`jobs` produces a real `string[]`**: antd `Select mode="tags"` binds an array to `config.jobs`. Confirmed by the test asserting `payload.config.jobs` `toEqual(['nightly', 'hourly'])`. The trailing comma in the typed input is dropped, matching the backend's `_non_empty_jobs` (no empty job names).
- Empty optional fields (`region`/`access_key_id`) are submitted as `undefined` and dropped by JSON serialization, so a `file://` connection sends only `{project_name, artifacts_uri, jobs}` — accepted by the backend (S3 validator only fires for `s3://`).
- Optional secret correctly models `file://` needing no credential.

### 3. dbt kept out of datasource contexts — correct
- `CONNECTION_KIND.dbt = 'orchestration'` and `DATASOURCE_CATEGORY.dbt = null` (connections.ts). Every run-target / suite / check-editor gate filters via `CONNECTION_KIND[c.type] === 'datasource'` (SuiteForm.tsx:53, ImportSuiteDrawer.tsx:47, Suites.tsx:112, SuiteNew.tsx:55), so dbt is never offered as a checkable datasource. `SOURCE_CATEGORY.dbt = 'Orchestration'` places it in the correct picker bucket. Consistent with CLAUDE.md §4 / ADR 0029.

### 4. Exhaustiveness — complete
All 7 `Record<ConnectionType, ...>` maps carry a dbt entry: `CONNECTION_KIND`, `DATASOURCE_CATEGORY`, `CONNECTION_TYPE_LABELS`, `TYPE_VISUAL`, `CONNECTION_FORM_SPECS`, `SOURCE_CATEGORY`, `CONNECTION_BLURB`. tsc enforces these; no runtime iteration over `CONNECTION_TYPES` builds a datasource list (datasource offers come from `CONNECTION_KIND` filtering, not enumeration), so adding dbt can't wrongly surface it anywhere.

### 5. Test quality — adequate
The new test (`ConnectionNew.test.tsx:56`) asserts both required behaviours: the jobs-array shape (`toEqual(['nightly', 'hourly'])`) and the no-secret path (Create succeeds and `payload.secret ?? '' === ''`). Because a required secret would block `onFinish`, the passing `mockCreate` call genuinely exercises `optionalSecret`.

### Low-confidence notes (below threshold — non-blocking, author's discretion)
- **Optional secret is scheme-blind (confidence ~55).** The credential is marked optional for *all* dbt connections, but it's only truly optional for `file://`; for `adls://`/`s3://` the artifacts-read credential is effectively required and the form won't prompt for it. This is a guidance gap, not a correctness bug — a missing credential fails loudly at the backend connectivity `test()`, not silently. Deriving requiredness from the `artifacts_uri` scheme would need a cross-field watch, which the current spec framework doesn't express; leaving it to backend validation is a reasonable tradeoff.
- **Test coverage gap (confidence ~40).** The test only covers the `file://` / no-secret happy path. An `s3://` variant (asserting `region`/`access_key_id` are submitted and a secret rides along) would lock in the other branch, but the backend already owns that validation and has its own tests.

Relevant files: `/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/frontend/src/components/connections/connectionFormSpec.ts`, `/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/frontend/src/components/connections/ConnectionTypeFields.tsx`, `/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/frontend/src/api/connections.ts`, `/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/backend/app/orchestration/dbt.py`.</result>

class SuiteNotification(Base):
    """Per-suite alert delivery config (one row per suite).

    Decides *whether* a suite's run outcomes are delivered (``enabled``), at what
    threshold (``alert_on``), and *where* — an optional per-suite Teams webhook
    referenced via ``webhook_secret_ref`` (the URL is token-bearing, so it lives
    in the SecretStore, never the DB), falling back to the workspace webhook when
    NULL. Suites with no row use the default policy (alert on warn+). The Teams
    publisher reads this when delivering (``alerting.teams``). Cascade-deleted
    with the suite.
    """

    __tablename__ = "suite_notifications"
    __table_args__ = (
        _in_check("alert_on", ALERT_ON_POLICIES, "alert_on_valid"),
        UniqueConstraint("suite_id", name="uq_suite_notifications_suite_id"),
    )

    id: Mapped[uuid.UUID] = _uuid_pk()
    suite_id: Mapped[uuid.UUID] = mapped_column(
        UUID(as_uuid=True), ForeignKey("suites.id", ondelete="CASCADE"), nullable=False
    )
    enabled: Mapped[bool] = mapped_column(Boolean, nullable=False, server_default=text("true"))
    # Delivery threshold (ADR 0005 tiers): 'fail' = fail/critical only, 'warn' =
    # warn+, 'always' = every terminal run. Default 'warn' matches the no-config
    # fallback (`notification_service.DEFAULT_ALERT_ON`) so saving a config
    # doesn't silently change the threshold. See `alerting.routing.route_for`.
    alert_on: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(16), nullable=False, server_default=text("'warn'"))
    # SecretStore key for the per-suite Teams webhook URL (NULL → workspace webhook).
    # The URL is a secret; only the ref is stored here.
    webhook_secret_ref: Mapped[str | None] = mapped_column(String(256))

    updated_at: Mapped[datetime] = _updated_at()

content.text: While #638 finishes CI, I want to confirm the #633 approach — it's genuinely a different class of change (a DB migration + backend + frontend), and I shouldn't guess at a schema. Here's the decision:

questions.question: #633 (per-suite Slack + email, 'same as Teams') is a schema-changing full-stack feature — the per-suite notification row currently holds only ONE Teams webhook; Slack/email are workspace-env-only. How should I proceed?

options.label: Full build now
options.description: Migration adds per-suite slack_webhook_secret_ref + email recipients to SuiteNotification; SlackPublisher + EmailPublisher resolve per-suite (workspace fallback); NotificationsPanel gains a Slack URL field + email-recipients field, mirroring Teams. Backward-compatible migration, its own PR (migration-safety + code review).
options.preview:
migration: +slack_webhook_secret_ref, +email_to (nullable)
service: upsert slack/email + SSRF allowlist hooks.slack.com
publishers: per-suite resolve, workspace fallback
API + NotificationsPanel: Slack URL + email recipients
(full-stack, 1 PR)

options.label: Contained items first, then #633

now: #635 dropdown, #634 auto-classify
then: #633 full-stack as its own PR

options.label: Slack only (lighter, no migration)

route per-suite webhook by host (Slack|Teams)
no migration, no email

1	"""Per-suite alert notification config (``suite_notifications``).
2	
3	Stores whether / where / at-what-threshold a suite's run outcomes are delivered.
4	The Teams webhook URL is token-bearing, so it's written through the SecretStore
5	and referenced by ``webhook_secret_ref`` (mirrors connection credentials) — never
6	plaintext in the DB. The Teams publisher reads this config when delivering.
7	
8	FastAPI-free like the sibling services: takes a ``Session`` (+ a ``SecretStore``
9	where credentials are involved), returns ORM models, raises ``DataQError``.
10	"""
11	
12	from __future__ import annotations
13	
14	import uuid
15	from urllib.parse import urlparse
16	
17	from sqlalchemy import select
18	from sqlalchemy.exc import IntegrityError
19	from sqlalchemy.orm import Session
20	
21	from backend.app.core.config import get_settings
22	from backend.app.core.errors import DataQError
23	from backend.app.core.logging import get_logger
24	from backend.app.core.secrets import SecretNotFoundError, SecretStore
25	from backend.app.db.models import ALERT_ON_POLICIES, SuiteNotification
26	
27	log = get_logger(__name__)
28	
29	# Threshold used for a suite with no config row — preserves the pre-config
30	# behaviour (alert on warn+). A suite opts into a stricter/looser policy by
31	# saving a config.
32	DEFAULT_ALERT_ON = "warn"
33	
34	
35	class InvalidAlertPolicyError(DataQError):
36	    """Raised when ``alert_on`` isn't one of the allowed policies."""
37	
38	    status_code = 422
39	    code = "alert_policy_invalid"
40	
41	
42	class InvalidWebhookError(DataQError):
43	    """Raised when a webhook URL isn't https or targets a non-allowlisted host."""
44	
45	    status_code = 422
46	    code = "webhook_invalid"
47	
48	
49	def allowed_webhook_hosts() -> tuple[str, ...]:
50	    """Host suffixes a per-suite Teams webhook URL may target (SSRF allowlist).
51	
52	    Sourced from the ``teams_webhook_allowed_hosts`` setting (comma-separated).
53	    """
54	    raw = get_settings().teams_webhook_allowed_hosts
55	    return tuple(host.strip().lower() for host in raw.split(",") if host.strip())
56	
57	
58	def is_allowed_webhook(url: str) -> bool:
59	    """True iff ``url`` is an https URL whose host is within the allowlist.
60	
61	    SSRF guard: the webhook is user-supplied and POSTed server-side, so the
62	    destination host is constrained to the configured Teams/Power-Automate set
63	    (an exact match or a subdomain of an allowed suffix).
64	    """
65	    parsed = urlparse(url)
66	    if parsed.scheme != "https" or not parsed.hostname:
67	        return False
68	    host = parsed.hostname.lower()
69	    return any(
70	        host == allowed or host.endswith(f".{allowed}") for allowed in allowed_webhook_hosts()
71	    )
72	
73	
74	def assert_allowed_webhook(url: str) -> None:
75	    """Raise ``InvalidWebhookError`` unless ``url`` passes :func:`is_allowed_webhook`."""
76	    if not is_allowed_webhook(url):
77	        raise InvalidWebhookError(
78	            "webhook must be an https URL on an allowed host",
79	            detail={"allowed_hosts": list(allowed_webhook_hosts())},
80	        )
81	
82	
83	def get_config(session: Session, suite_id: uuid.UUID) -> SuiteNotification | None:
84	    """The suite's notification config, or None if it has never been saved."""
85	    return session.scalars(
86	        select(SuiteNotification).where(SuiteNotification.suite_id == suite_id)
87	    ).first()
88	
89	
90	def upsert_config(
91	    session: Session,
92	    *,
93	    suite_id: uuid.UUID,
94	    enabled: bool,
95	    alert_on: str,
96	    webhook: str | None,
97	    secret_store: SecretStore,
98	) -> SuiteNotification:
99	    """Create or update a suite's notification config.
100	
101	    ``webhook`` is tri-state: ``None`` leaves the stored webhook unchanged, ``""``
102	    clears it (fall back to the workspace webhook), and a non-empty value is
103	    written through the SecretStore (the ref is derived from the row id, like a
104	    connection credential).
105	    """
106	    if alert_on not in ALERT_ON_POLICIES:
107	        raise InvalidAlertPolicyError(
108	            "invalid alert policy",
109	            detail={"alert_on": alert_on, "allowed": list(ALERT_ON_POLICIES)},
110	        )
111	    if webhook:  # non-empty → https + allowlisted host (token-bearing, sent server-side)

116	            # SAVEPOINT so a concurrent first-write losing the unique race
117	            # (uq_suite_notifications_suite_id) rolls back just this insert, not the
118	            # whole transaction. flush() assigns the id for the secret_ref below and
119	            # surfaces the conflict here.
120	            with session.begin_nested():
121	                config = SuiteNotification(suite_id=suite_id, enabled=enabled, alert_on=alert_on)

123	                session.flush()
124	        except IntegrityError:
125	            # A concurrent request won the insert — update its row instead of
126	            # 500-ing on the unique violation (#384).
127	            config = get_config(session, suite_id)
128	            if config is None:  # pragma: no cover — the winner's row must exist post-rollback
129	                raise
130	            config.enabled = enabled
131	            config.alert_on = alert_on
132	    else:
133	        config.enabled = enabled
134	        config.alert_on = alert_on
135	
136	    cleared_secret_ref: str | None = None
137	    if webhook is not None:
138	        if webhook == "":
139	            # Clearing the per-suite webhook — drop the orphaned secret too (#372),
140	            # but only AFTER the commit (below), so a rolled-back commit can't leave
141	            # the row pointing at an already-deleted secret.
142	            cleared_secret_ref = config.webhook_secret_ref
143	            config.webhook_secret_ref = None
144	        else:
145	            # Mint a UNIQUE ref per fresh set (not a stable `suite-notif-{id}`): the
146	            # clear path now soft-deletes the secret (#372), and Key Vault refuses to
147	            # re-`set` a soft-deleted *name* (409 until purge/recover, which the app
148	            # deliberately can't do) — so reusing the name on a clear→re-set of the
149	            # same config would 500. A rotation (ref still set) reuses the live name
150	            # (a new version, no conflict).
151	            secret_ref = (
152	                config.webhook_secret_ref or f"suite-notif-{config.id}-{uuid.uuid4().hex[:12]}"
153	            )
154	            secret_store.set(secret_ref, webhook)
155	            config.webhook_secret_ref = secret_ref
156	
157	    session.commit()
158	    session.refresh(config)
159	    # Post-commit, fail-soft: remove the cleared webhook's now-orphaned secret (#372).
160	    if cleared_secret_ref:
161	        secret_store.delete(cleared_secret_ref)
162	    log.info("suite_notification_saved", suite_id=str(suite_id), enabled=enabled, alert_on=alert_on)
163	    return config
164	
165	
166	def delete_config(session: Session, suite_id: uuid.UUID, *, secret_store: SecretStore) -> bool:
167	    """Delete a suite's config (revert to defaults). Returns whether a row existed."""
168	    config = get_config(session, suite_id)
169	    if config is None:
170	        return False
171	    secret_ref = config.webhook_secret_ref
172	    session.delete(config)
173	    session.commit()
174	    # Best-effort remove the orphaned per-suite webhook secret (#372), fail-soft.
175	    if secret_ref:
176	        secret_store.delete(secret_ref)
177	    log.info("suite_notification_deleted", suite_id=str(suite_id))
178	    return True
179	
180	
181	def resolve_webhook(
182	    config: SuiteNotification | None,
183	    *,
184	    secret_store: SecretStore,
185	    workspace_secret_name: str | None,
186	) -> str | None:
187	    """The webhook URL to deliver to: the per-suite one, else the workspace one.
188	
189	    Returns ``None`` when neither resolves (delivery is then skipped). A missing
190	    secret is logged (by ref/name, never the value) and falls through.
191	    """
192	    ref = config.webhook_secret_ref if config is not None else None
193	    if ref:
194	        try:
195	            return secret_store.get(ref)
196	        except SecretNotFoundError:
197	            log.warning("suite_webhook_unresolved", secret_ref=ref)
198	    if workspace_secret_name:
199	        try:
200	            return secret_store.get(workspace_secret_name)
201	        except SecretNotFoundError:
202	            log.warning("teams_webhook_unresolved", secret_name=workspace_secret_name)
203	    return None

"""widen orchestration value-sets + trigger-dedup predicate for the dbt provider

Adds ``dbt`` as a third `OrchestrationProvider` (ADR 0029, #611). dbt is an
orchestration provider, not a datasource (CLAUDE.md §4), so it joins the same
value-sets ADF/Airflow live in. Five widenings, all **additive** (permit one more
value) and therefore backward-compatible — old code that never emits ``dbt`` is
unaffected, and no existing row can violate a widened constraint:

1. ``ck_connections_type_valid``      — allow a ``dbt`` connection row.
2. ``uq_connections_orchestrator_type_env`` — one dbt connection per env (as ADF/Airflow).
3. ``ck_pipeline_runs_provider_valid`` — allow ``provider='dbt'`` pipeline runs.
4. ``ck_trigger_bindings_provider_valid`` — allow ``provider='dbt'`` trigger bindings.
5. ``uq_runs_suite_triggered_by``      — extend the trigger-dedup predicate to ``dbt:%``.

Tested up + down locally. Raw SQL (exact constraint/index names) mirrors the #308
dedup-index migration; kept in sync with the model constraints in `db/models.py`
and `orchestration_service._ORCH_TRIGGER_PREDICATE`.

**Lock footprint (deliberate):** the two index rebuilds `DROP` + non-concurrently
`CREATE UNIQUE INDEX`, taking a brief ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock on `connections` and
`runs`, and the CHECK re-adds full-scan-validate — all in one transaction. This is
the *gap-free* choice on purpose: staying transactional means a concurrent
orchestration webhook's `ON CONFLICT (uq_runs_suite_triggered_by)` blocks on the
lock and resumes against the rebuilt index, never hitting a "no matching unique
constraint" window that a `CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY` rebuild would expose. At the
current demo/harness table sizes the lock is sub-second, so this is the right
trade-off. If `connections`/`runs` ever grow large, split the index rebuilds into a
`postgresql_concurrently=True` migration (per the note on `aa33d80c2158`) and the
CHECKs into `NOT VALID` + `VALIDATE CONSTRAINT`.

**Downgrade window:** this PR also wires `DbtProvider` into the registry (no feature
flag), so a `dbt` connection/webhook can land minutes after deploy — ``downgrade``
is only safe in the brief gap *before any dbt activity*, not "until the service
ships". After that, roll forward, not back (see ``downgrade``).
"""

from collections.abc import Sequence

from alembic import op

"""per-suite Slack webhook + email recipients on suite_notifications

Adds per-suite overrides for the Slack and email channels (#633), mirroring the
existing per-suite Teams ``webhook_secret_ref``. Both columns are **nullable and
additive** — NULL means "fall back to the workspace-level config" (a rotated Slack
webhook / ``EMAIL_TO``), so every existing row keeps its current behaviour and old
code that never reads the columns is unaffected. Fully backward-compatible.

* ``slack_webhook_secret_ref`` — SecretStore key for the per-suite Slack webhook
  URL (token-bearing, so only the ref is stored), same shape as the Teams ref.
* ``email_recipients`` — comma-separated addresses (not a secret), stored inline.

Tested up + down locally. ``downgrade`` drops both columns; safe because the
publishers treat a missing per-suite override as "use the workspace fallback", so
rolling back only loses the per-suite overrides (delivery continues on workspace
config). No data migration.
"""

from collections.abc import Sequence

import sqlalchemy as sa
from alembic import op

    )
    op.add_column(

def downgrade() -> None:

    op.drop_column("suite_notifications", "slack_webhook_secret_ref")

input.old_string:
def upsert_config(
    session: Session,
    *,
    suite_id: uuid.UUID,
    enabled: bool,
    alert_on: str,
    webhook: str | None,
    secret_store: SecretStore,
) -> SuiteNotification:
    """Create or update a suite's notification config.

    ``webhook`` is tri-state: ``None`` leaves the stored webhook unchanged, ``""``
    clears it (fall back to the workspace webhook), and a non-empty value is
    written through the SecretStore (the ref is derived from the row id, like a
    connection credential).
    """
    if alert_on not in ALERT_ON_POLICIES:
        raise InvalidAlertPolicyError(
            "invalid alert policy",
            detail={"alert_on": alert_on, "allowed": list(ALERT_ON_POLICIES)},
        )
    if webhook:  # non-empty → https + allowlisted host (token-bearing, sent server-side)
        assert_allowed_webhook(webhook)
    config = get_config(session, suite_id)
    if config is None:
        try:
            # SAVEPOINT so a concurrent first-write losing the unique race
            # (uq_suite_notifications_suite_id) rolls back just this insert, not the
            # whole transaction. flush() assigns the id for the secret_ref below and
            # surfaces the conflict here.
            with session.begin_nested():
                config = SuiteNotification(suite_id=suite_id, enabled=enabled, alert_on=alert_on)
                session.add(config)
                session.flush()
        except IntegrityError:
            # A concurrent request won the insert — update its row instead of
            # 500-ing on the unique violation (#384).
            config = get_config(session, suite_id)
            if config is None:  # pragma: no cover — the winner's row must exist post-rollback
                raise
            config.enabled = enabled
            config.alert_on = alert_on
    else:
        config.enabled = enabled
        config.alert_on = alert_on

    cleared_secret_ref: str | None = None
    if webhook is not None:
        if webhook == "":
            # Clearing the per-suite webhook — drop the orphaned secret too (#372),
            # but only AFTER the commit (below), so a rolled-back commit can't leave
            # the row pointing at an already-deleted secret.
            cleared_secret_ref = config.webhook_secret_ref
            config.webhook_secret_ref = None
        else:
            # Mint a UNIQUE ref per fresh set (not a stable `suite-notif-{id}`): the
            # clear path now soft-deletes the secret (#372), and Key Vault refuses to
            # re-`set` a soft-deleted *name* (409 until purge/recover, which the app
            # deliberately can't do) — so reusing the name on a clear→re-set of the
            # same config would 500. A rotation (ref still set) reuses the live name
            # (a new version, no conflict).
            secret_ref = (
                config.webhook_secret_ref or f"suite-notif-{config.id}-{uuid.uuid4().hex[:12]}"
            )
            secret_store.set(secret_ref, webhook)
            config.webhook_secret_ref = secret_ref

    session.commit()
    session.refresh(config)
    # Post-commit, fail-soft: remove the cleared webhook's now-orphaned secret (#372).
    if cleared_secret_ref:
        secret_store.delete(cleared_secret_ref)
    log.info("suite_notification_saved", suite_id=str(suite_id), enabled=enabled, alert_on=alert_on)
    return config
input.new_string:
def _apply_secret_webhook(
    value: str | None,
    current_ref: str | None,
    *,
    ref_prefix: str,
    config_id: uuid.UUID,
    secret_store: SecretStore,
) -> tuple[str | None, str | None]:
    """Apply a tri-state webhook change to a secret-backed ref column.

    Returns ``(new_ref, cleared_ref)``. ``value`` is tri-state: ``None`` leaves the
    ref unchanged; ``""`` clears it (``new_ref=None``, ``cleared_ref`` = the old ref
    to soft-delete AFTER commit, #372); a non-empty value is written through the
    SecretStore under a UNIQUE fresh ref (avoids Key Vault soft-deleted-name reuse)
    or, on rotation, the live ref (a new version). The ``set`` happens here, before
    the caller's commit — matching the connection-credential write-through.
    """
    if value is None:
        return current_ref, None
    if value == "":
        return None, current_ref
    ref = current_ref or f"{ref_prefix}-{config_id}-{uuid.uuid4().hex[:12]}"
    secret_store.set(ref, value)
    return ref, None


def upsert_config(
    session: Session,
    *,
    suite_id: uuid.UUID,
    enabled: bool,
    alert_on: str,
    webhook: str | None,
    slack_webhook: str | None = None,
    email_recipients: str | None = None,
    secret_store: SecretStore,
) -> SuiteNotification:
    """Create or update a suite's notification config.

    Each channel override is **tri-state**: ``None`` leaves it unchanged, ``""``
    clears it (fall back to the workspace-level config), and a non-empty value sets
    it. ``webhook`` (Teams) and ``slack_webhook`` are token-bearing → written through
    the SecretStore under a per-row ref (#633); ``email_recipients`` is a plain
    comma-separated string stored inline (addresses aren't secrets).
    """
    if alert_on not in ALERT_ON_POLICIES:
        raise InvalidAlertPolicyError(
            "invalid alert policy",
            detail={"alert_on": alert_on, "allowed": list(ALERT_ON_POLICIES)},
        )
    # Validate every supplied value up front (before touching the DB / SecretStore).
    if webhook:
        assert_allowed_webhook(webhook)
    if slack_webhook:
        assert_allowed_slack_webhook(slack_webhook)
    if email_recipients:
        assert_valid_recipients(email_recipients)

    config = get_config(session, suite_id)
    if config is None:
        try:
            # SAVEPOINT so a concurrent first-write losing the unique race
            # (uq_suite_notifications_suite_id) rolls back just this insert, not the
            # whole transaction. flush() assigns the id for the secret refs below and
            # surfaces the conflict here.
            with session.begin_nested():
                config = SuiteNotification(suite_id=suite_id, enabled=enabled, alert_on=alert_on)
                session.add(config)
                session.flush()
        except IntegrityError:
            # A concurrent request won the insert — update its row instead of
            # 500-ing on the unique violation (#384).
            config = get_config(session, suite_id)
            if config is None:  # pragma: no cover — the winner's row must exist post-rollback
                raise
            config.enabled = enabled
            config.alert_on = alert_on
    else:
        config.enabled = enabled
        config.alert_on = alert_on

    # Teams + Slack webhooks — secret-backed, tri-state; cleared refs are soft-deleted
    # after commit (#372) so a rolled-back commit can't orphan a live ref.
    config.webhook_secret_ref, cleared_teams = _apply_secret_webhook(
        webhook, config.webhook_secret_ref, ref_prefix="suite-notif", config_id=config.id, secret_store=secret_store
    )
    config.slack_webhook_secret_ref, cleared_slack = _apply_secret_webhook(
        slack_webhook,
        config.slack_webhook_secret_ref,
        ref_prefix="suite-notif-slack",
        config_id=config.id,
        secret_store=secret_store,
    )
    # Email recipients — inline, tri-state ("" clears to NULL → workspace fallback).
    if email_recipients is not None:
        config.email_recipients = email_recipients or None

    session.commit()
    session.refresh(config)
    # Post-commit, fail-soft: remove any now-orphaned webhook secrets (#372).
    for cleared in (cleared_teams, cleared_slack):
        if cleared:
            secret_store.delete(cleared)
    log.info("suite_notification_saved", suite_id=str(suite_id), enabled=enabled, alert_on=alert_on)
    return config

def _build_publisher() -> ResultPublisher:
    """A composite over every channel (Teams · Slack · email). Each child resolves
    its secret + per-suite policy per run (so rotation is picked up) and stays a
    quiet no-op until configured; the registry only wires the store + names, never
    reads a secret."""
    settings = get_settings()
    store = get_secret_store()
    return CompositePublisher(
        [
            TeamsPublisher(
                secret_store=store,
                workspace_secret_name=settings.teams_webhook_secret_name,
            ),
            SlackPublisher(

    """Sends a run's report over SMTP (STARTTLS) to the per-suite recipients, else

        the run is below the suite's threshold. Raises on an SMTP error — the
        composite layer isolates it so a flaky mailer can't fail the run or block
        the other channels.
        """
        if not (
            self._recipients and self._username and self._password_secret_name and self._sender
        ):
            return

        if config is not None and not config.enabled:

        route = route_for(report, policy)
        if not route.should_send:

        except SecretNotFoundError:

        context = ssl.create_default_context()

            server.starttls(context=context)

            server.send_message(message)
        log.info(
            "email_alert_sent",

input.new_string:
Quiet no-op when the SMTP transport is unconfigured, no recipients resolve
        (neither per-suite nor workspace), the suite disabled alerting, or the run is
        below the suite's threshold. Raises on an SMTP error — the composite layer
        isolates it so a flaky mailer can't fail the run or block the other channels.
        """
        # The SMTP transport is workspace-level and mandatory (recipients are per-suite).
        if not (self._username and self._password_secret_name and self._sender):
            return
        config = notification_service.get_config(session, report.suite_id)
        if config is not None and not config.enabled:
            return
        policy = config.alert_on if config is not None else notification_service.DEFAULT_ALERT_ON
        route = route_for(report, policy)
        if not route.should_send:
            return
        recipients = notification_service.resolve_email_recipients(
            config, workspace_recipients=self._recipients
        )
        if not recipients:
            return  # no per-suite override and no workspace EMAIL_TO → no-op
        try:
            password = self._secret_store.get(self._password_secret_name)
        except SecretNotFoundError:
            log.warning("email_password_unresolved", secret_name=self._password_secret_name)
            return

        message = EmailMessage()
        message["Subject"] = render_subject(report)
        message["From"] = self._sender
        message["To"] = ", ".join(recipients)
        message.set_content(render_text_body(report))
        message.add_alternative(render_html_body(report), subtype="html")

        context = ssl.create_default_context()
        with smtplib.SMTP(self._smtp_host, self._smtp_port, timeout=self._timeout) as server:
            server.starttls(context=context)
            server.login(self._username, password)
            server.send_message(message)
        log.info(
            "email_alert_sent",
            run_id=str(report.run_id),
            suite=report.suite_name,
            recipients=len(recipients),
            worst_severity=report.worst_severity,
        )

    webhook: str | None = None
